metadata.title: Why Horror Games Work: The Neuroscience of Fear
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
tags: Why Horror Games Work: The Neuroscience
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Horror games exploit an evolved threat-response system, described as a firmware bug in humans. Outlast's battery mechanic conditions fear the way Pavlov conditioned his dogs. Designers who treat fear as vibes get unpredictable, inconsistent results. Horror design is applied neuroscience, not merely storytelling.

artifactLines: Horror games exploit an evolved threat-response system, described as a firmware bug in humans.
Outlast's battery mechanic conditions fear the way Pavlov conditioned his dogs.
Designers who treat fear as vibes get unpredictable, inconsistent results.
Horror design is applied neuroscience, not merely storytelling.

beats.narration_text: The framing that horror games exploit a firmware bug in Homo sapiens, and that Outlast's battery mechanic is essentially Pavlovian conditioning, makes me want to name the mechanisms precisely. Help me: (1) identify what unconditioned response the battery drain gets paired with to become a learned fear cue; (2) explain why treating fear as 'vibes' yields unpredictable results; (3) list two other horror mechanics that map onto specific conditioning or threat-response principles.
